page.content: The first table presents several studies examining the impact of early life experiences on DNA methylation, with the objective of determining the effect of childhood SES on genome-wide methylation in adulthood using Retrospective Peripheral blood DNA from 40 males assessed at 45 years, revealing 666 gene promoters ↑ and 586 promoters ↓ methylation in high vs low childhood SES, indicating variations in childhood SES cause changes in genome-wide methylation in adulthood, particularly in genes related to extra and intracellular signaling, DNA signaling, and metabolic signaling, referenced as [79].

lines.text: The first table presents several studies examining the impact of early life experiences on DNA methylation, with the objective
of determining the effect of childhood SES on genome-wide methylation in adulthood using Retrospective Peripheral blood
DNA from 40 males assessed at 45 years, revealing 666 gene promoters ↑ and 586 promoters ↓ methylation in high vs low
childhood SES, indicating variations in childhood SES cause changes in genome-wide methylation in adulthood, particularly in
genes related to extra and intracellular signaling, DNA signaling, and metabolic signaling, referenced as [79].

Determine the effect of childhood abuse on genome-wide DNA methylationRetrospectiveHippocampusCA + suicide vs no CA + suicideAssessed: adulthoodN = 41 MDifferentially methylated promoters in CA vs no CA were spread across the genome248 (68.5 %) promoters ↑ methylation in CA114 (31.5 %) promoters ↓ methylation in CA↑ methylation associated w ↓ mRNA expChildhood abuse causes alteration in the methylation of gene promoters and mRNA exp in adulthood specifically genes involved in neuronal plasticity[93]

Determine the effect of childhood abuse in methylation status of immune system and cytokine regulationRetrospectivePeripheral blood DNAPTSD + CA, PTSD + no CA, C + CA, C + no CAAssessed: adulthoodN = 110↑ global methylation in PTSD vs C↔ methylation due to CA in PTSD or CGene specific associations with found in BDNF, HSF1, TLR8 for PTSD and CA↑plasma TNFα in CA vs no CAChildhood abuse early in life can alter global and gene specific DNA methylation patterns specifically involved in immune dysregulation[81]

ScorePresence of surface DNAInvasion into the templateDegree of neutrophil degeneration
1DNA adherent to some surfaces of the templateSlight invasion into the template near a fold or defect in the templateMost invading neutrophils non-degenerative
2DNA adherent to most surfaces of the templateSlight invasion into the templateMany invading neutrophils non-degenerative
3Dense DNA layer adherent to some surfaces of the templateModerate invasion with reduced invasion toward the center of the templateOccasional invading neutrophils non-degenerative
4Dense DNA layer adherent to most surfaces of the templateModerate invasion throughout the thickness of the membraneFew non-degenerating neutrophils not deeply invaded
5Conspicuous, dense DNA layer adherent to the surfaces of the templateDense invasion throughout the thickness of the membraneDiffuse degenerating neutrophils near the surface of defect in the template.

page.content: Another study investigated the effect of early environment on genome-wide methylation levels, utilizing Retrospective Peripheral blood DNA from 28 participants assessed at a mean age of 8.25 years, highlighting differential methylation of 914 of 26,214 CpG sites from 838 gene promoters, with ↑ methylation of 744 promoters and ↓ methylation of 94 promoters in institutionalized groups, suggesting early environmental alterations impact genes important for cellular signaling and immune response, referenced as [77]. A study on childhood abuse and DNA methylation analyzed Retrospective Hippocampus data from 41 males assessed in adulthood, showing 248 (68.5 %) promoters ↑ methylation and 114 (31.5 %) promoters ↓ methylation in childhood abuse cases, indicating childhood abuse alters gene promoter methylation and mRNA expression related to neuronal plasticity, referenced as [93]. Furthermore, the impact of childhood abuse on PTSD was examined using Retrospective Peripheral blood DNA from 61 individuals assessed at mean ages of 39.6 years for CA and 43.69 years for no CA, revealing ↑ methylation in transcripts of the PTSD + CA group (11.78 %) compared to PTSD + no CA (0.8 %), with 14 differentially methylated transcripts, suggesting that childhood abuse alters methylation in PTSD, specifically impacting CNS development, referenced as [80]. Lastly, the study on childhood abuse in methylation status of immune system and cytokine regulation involved 110 participants assessed in adulthood, indicating ↑ global methylation in PTSD vs C, with gene-specific associations found in BDNF, HSF1, TLR8 for PTSD and CA, and ↑ plasma TNFα in CA vs no CA, concluding that childhood abuse can significantly alter global and gene-specific DNA methylation patterns related to immune dysregulation, referenced as [81]. The second table assesses the score of DNA presence, invasion into the template, and degree of neutrophil degeneration, with a score of 1 indicating DNA adherent to some surfaces of the template, slight invasion near a fold or defect, with most invading neutrophils being non-degenerative; a score of 2 shows DNA adherent to most surfaces, slight invasion, and many non-degenerative neutrophils; a score of 3 indicates a dense DNA layer on some surfaces, moderate invasion with reduced invasion toward the center, with occasional non-degenerative neutrophils; a score of 4 reflects a dense DNA layer on most surfaces, moderate invasion throughout the membrane thickness, and few non-degenerating neutrophils; a score of 5 denotes a conspicuous, dense DNA layer on the template surfaces, dense invasion throughout, and diffuse degenerating neutrophils near the surface defect.

lines.text: Another study investigated the effect of early environment on genome-wide methylation levels, utilizing Retrospective
Peripheral blood DNA from 28 participants assessed at a mean age of 8.25 years, highlighting differential methylation of 914 of
26,214 CpG sites from 838 gene promoters, with ↑ methylation of 744 promoters and ↓ methylation of 94 promoters in
institutionalized groups, suggesting early environmental alterations impact genes important for cellular signaling and immune
response, referenced as [77]. A study on childhood abuse and DNA methylation analyzed Retrospective Hippocampus data from
41 males assessed in adulthood, showing 248 (68.5 %) promoters ↑ methylation and 114 (31.5 %) promoters ↓ methylation in
childhood abuse cases, indicating childhood abuse alters gene promoter methylation and mRNA expression related to neuronal
plasticity, referenced as [93]. Furthermore, the impact of childhood abuse on PTSD was examined using Retrospective
Peripheral blood DNA from 61 individuals assessed at mean ages of 39.6 years for CA and 43.69 years for no CA, revealing ↑
methylation in transcripts of the PTSD + CA group (11.78 %) compared to PTSD + no CA (0.8 %), with 14 differentially
methylated transcripts, suggesting that childhood abuse alters methylation in PTSD, specifically impacting CNS development,
referenced as [80]. Lastly, the study on childhood abuse in methylation status of immune system and cytokine regulation
involved 110 participants assessed in adulthood, indicating ↑ global methylation in PTSD vs C, with gene-specific associations
found in BDNF, HSF1, TLR8 for PTSD and CA, and ↑ plasma TNFα in CA vs no CA, concluding that childhood abuse can
significantly alter global and gene-specific DNA methylation patterns related to immune dysregulation, referenced as [81]. The
second table assesses the score of DNA presence, invasion into the template, and degree of neutrophil degeneration, with a
score of 1 indicating DNA adherent to some surfaces of the template, slight invasion near a fold or defect, with most invading
neutrophils being non-degenerative; a score of 2 shows DNA adherent to most surfaces, slight invasion, and many non-
degenerative neutrophils; a score of 3 indicates a dense DNA layer on some surfaces, moderate invasion with reduced invasion
toward the center, with occasional non-degenerative neutrophils; a score of 4 reflects a dense DNA layer on most surfaces,
moderate invasion throughout the membrane thickness, and few non-degenerating neutrophils; a score of 5 denotes a
conspicuous, dense DNA layer on the template surfaces, dense invasion throughout, and diffuse degenerating neutrophils near
the surface defect.
